interactions.summary: Jiang introduces Paradise Lost as the greatest English poem and says Milton was a secret-society member whose poetry embeds the hidden truth.

A student asks whether there is a true God if this world is made by the false God; Jiang answers that the Monad is true but does not directly interfere, either because it leaves humans free to return or because human error expands its imagination.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang finishes the Monad/imagination answer and frames Paradise Lost as the British imperial epic, a soul-forming text whose secrets are visible only to those who already know the secret.

speaker_notes.note: Real student question: whether the system has a true God if this world is from the false God.

claims.claim: Jiang says Paradise Lost encodes secret-society truth and that proper poetic analysis can reveal what members embedded in literature.
claims.temporal_scope: Literary method claim in the 2025-09-05 lecture.

claims.claim: In answer to a student, Jiang identifies the Monad as the true God and says it may not interfere because humans must freely choose spiritual return.
A second explanation, attributed to Dante, says the eternal perfect Monad lacks imagination and permits flawed humans because mistakes and evil expand universal imagination.
Milton is presented as a blind prophetic free thinker and Paradise Lost as the foundational text of many secret societies.
The plot of Paradise Lost is introduced as Milton's reimagining of the fall, with Satan turning from defeated rebel to the one who volunteers to go to Eden.

signature_moments.moment: The true God does not force return; the soul must choose the path back.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you want to return to him then you have the choice
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It keeps the lecture's salvation model from becoming another external command.

signature_moments.moment: Human evil and mistake become the imagination engine of the universe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: by committing evil by making mistakes we expand the imagination of the universe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why transgression later becomes spiritually dangerous but necessary.

signature_moments.moment: Paradise Lost hides secrets that only people who already know the secret can see.
signature_moments.source_phrase: only if you understand the secrets yourself can you see the secrets
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the lecture's hermeneutic: poetry is an encoded initiation device.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Milton's epic poem, treated by Jiang as a foundational secret-society text that encodes hidden cosmology.
The false God whose prison-world raises the student question about why the true God does not intervene.
The growth of the Monad through human flaw, mistake, evil, recognition, and adjustment.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR has duplicated and garbled lines near “British Empire/Anglo-American Empire”; the Paradise Lost framing remains clear.
